Sueyoshi-gū] [[Category:new pages]] {{Shinto side}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sueyoshi-gū}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]]]] [[Category:mm]] == Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} ==References== [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa and forks]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa and forks]] [[Category:Cer 3]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21 (2)]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:pgoodbye $1]][[Category:new pages]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:kk]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]]{{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}}[[en:Sueyoshi-gū]] [[ja:末吉宮]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:new pages]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sueyoshi-gū | image = [[File:Sueyoshi Park Naha Okinawa Japan27s3.jpg|270px|Haiden]]
Haiden | location = 1-8 Sueyoshi-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 48.6 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 50.6 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Keitai era (1450–1457) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | other names = Shurishadan | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = November 23 | rituals = }} [[File:Sueyoshi-gū Honden.JPG|thumb|250px|main hall
Designated as a National Treasure in 1936, destroyed during the Pacific War, and rebuilt in 1972.]] [[File:Sueyoshigu.jpg|thumb|260x260px|View of Haiden from the side of the shrine office]] {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|26.230167|127.714056}}|caption=Sueyoshi-gū|width=256}} '''Sueyoshi-gū''' (末吉宮, Sueyoshi-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within [[Sueyoshi Park]] in [[Sueyoshi-cho]], [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are the [[Kumano Gongen]]: *'''[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]''' (いざなみのみこと) *'''[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]''' (はやたまをのみこと) *'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]''' (ことさかをのみこと) Additionally, the following deities are enshrined separately: *'''Tsuchi Mioya Kami''' (つちみおやかみ) *'''Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひこのみこと) *'''Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひめのみこと) *'''Ubusuna Kami''' (うぶすなのかみ) == History == According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Daikosan Manju-ji Temple, Sueyoshi-gū was founded during the [[Keitai era]] (1450–1457). The legend states that during the reign of King [[Shō Taikyū]], a monk named Tsuruo, who had been training in Japan, made a vow to visit [[Kumano]] upon his successful return to Ryukyu. However, the king did not permit his travel. Tsuruo then had a divine dream where he was told to call out towards the northern mountains to find a sacred site. Following the dream's instructions, he found a spiritual site and reported it to the king, who then established the shrine. This legend is also mentioned in ''[[Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unranked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unranked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} ==References== [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]]{{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}}[[en:Sueyoshi-gū]] [[ja:末吉宮]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]]

Sueyoshi-gū] [[Category:Pages to be written]] {{Shinto side}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sueyoshi-gū}} [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]]]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] == Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} ==References== [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]]{{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}}[[en:Sueyoshi-gū]] [[ja:末吉宮]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:qq]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sueyoshi-gū | image = [[File:Sueyoshi Park Naha Okinawa Japan27s3.jpg|270px|Haiden]]
Haiden | location = 1-8 Sueyoshi-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 48.6 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 50.6 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Keitai era (1450–1457) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | other names = Shurishadan | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = November 23 | rituals = }} [[File:Sueyoshi-gū Honden.JPG|thumb|250px|main hall
Designated as a National Treasure in 1936, destroyed during the Pacific War, and rebuilt in 1972.]] [[File:Sueyoshigu.jpg|thumb|260x260px|View of Haiden from the side of the shrine office]] {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|26.230167|127.714056}}|caption=Sueyoshi-gū|width=256}} '''Sueyoshi-gū''' (末吉宮, Sueyoshi-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within [[Sueyoshi Park]] in [[Sueyoshi-cho]], [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are the [[Kumano Gongen]]: *'''[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]''' (いざなみのみこと) *'''[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]''' (はやたまをのみこと) *'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]''' (ことさかをのみこと) Additionally, the following deities are enshrined separately: *'''Tsuchi Mioya Kami''' (つちみおやかみ) *'''Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひこのみこと) *'''Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひめのみこと) *'''Ubusuna Kami''' (うぶすなのかみ) == History == According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Daikosan Manju-ji Temple, Sueyoshi-gū was founded during the [[Keitai era]] (1450–1457). The legend states that during the reign of King [[Shō Taikyū]], a monk named Tsuruo, who had been training in Japan, made a vow to visit [[Kumano]] upon his successful return to Ryukyu. However, the king did not permit his travel. Tsuruo then had a divine dream where he was told to call out towards the northern mountains to find a sacred site. Following the dream's instructions, he found a spiritual site and reported it to the king, who then established the shrine. This legend is also mentioned in ''[[Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unranked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unranked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - Sueyoshi-gū] [[Category:Pages to be written]] {{Shinto side}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sueyoshi-gū}} [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]]]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] == Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} ==References== [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]]{{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}}[[en:Sueyoshi-gū]] [[ja:末吉宮]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]]

Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - Sueyoshi-gū] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] {{Shinto side}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sueyoshi-gū}} [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]]]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] == Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} ==References== [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]]{{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}}[[en:Sueyoshi-gū]] [[ja:末吉宮]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sueyoshi-gū | image = [[File:Sueyoshi Park Naha Okinawa Japan27s3.jpg|270px|Haiden]]
Haiden | location = 1-8 Sueyoshi-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 48.6 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 50.6 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Keitai era (1450–1457) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | other names = Shurishadan | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = November 23 | rituals = }} [[File:Sueyoshi-gū Honden.JPG|thumb|250px|main hall
Designated as a National Treasure in 1936, destroyed during the Pacific War, and rebuilt in 1972.]] [[File:Sueyoshigu.jpg|thumb|260x260px|View of Haiden from the side of the shrine office]] {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|26.230167|127.714056}}|caption=Sueyoshi-gū|width=256}} '''Sueyoshi-gū''' (末吉宮, Sueyoshi-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within [[Sueyoshi Park]] in [[Sueyoshi-cho]], [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are the [[Kumano Gongen]]: *'''[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]''' (いざなみのみこと) *'''[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]''' (はやたまをのみこと) *'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]''' (ことさかをのみこと) Additionally, the following deities are enshrined separately: *'''Tsuchi Mioya Kami''' (つちみおやかみ) *'''Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひこのみこと) *'''Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひめのみこと) *'''Ubusuna Kami''' (うぶすなのかみ) == History == According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Daikosan Manju-ji Temple, Sueyoshi-gū was founded during the [[Keitai era]] (1450–1457). The legend states that during the reign of King [[Shō Taikyū]], a monk named Tsuruo, who had been training in Japan, made a vow to visit [[Kumano]] upon his successful return to Ryukyu. However, the king did not permit his travel. Tsuruo then had a divine dream where he was told to call out towards the northern mountains to find a sacred site. Following the dream's instructions, he found a spiritual site and reported it to the king, who then established the shrine. This legend is also mentioned in ''[[Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unranked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unranked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} ==References== [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]]{{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}}[[en:Sueyoshi-gū]] [[ja:末吉宮]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:qq]]

Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - Sueyoshi-gū] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] {{Shinto side}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sueyoshi-gū}} [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]]]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] == Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} ==References== [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]]{{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}}[[en:Sueyoshi-gū]] [[ja:末吉宮]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:qq]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sueyoshi-gū | image = [[File:Sueyoshi Park Naha Okinawa Japan27s3.jpg|270px|Haiden]]
Haiden | location = 1-8 Sueyoshi-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 48.6 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 50.6 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Keitai era (1450–1457) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | other names = Shurishadan | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = November 23 | rituals = }} [[File:Sueyoshi-gū Honden.JPG|thumb|250px|main hall
Designated as a National Treasure in 1936, destroyed during the Pacific War, and rebuilt in 1972.]] [[File:Sueyoshigu.jpg|thumb|260x260px|View of Haiden from the side of the shrine office]] {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|26.230167|127.714056}}|caption=Sueyoshi-gū|width=256}} '''Sueyoshi-gū''' (末吉宮, Sueyoshi-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within [[Sueyoshi Park]] in [[Sueyoshi-cho]], [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are the [[Kumano Gongen]]: *'''[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]''' (いざなみのみこと) *'''[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]''' (はやたまをのみこと) *'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]''' (ことさかをのみこと) Additionally, the following deities are enshrined separately: *'''Tsuchi Mioya Kami''' (つちみおやかみ) *'''Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひこのみこと) *'''Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひめのみこと) *'''Ubusuna Kami''' (うぶすなのかみ) == History == According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Daikosan Manju-ji Temple, Sueyoshi-gū was founded during the [[Keitai era]] (1450–1457). The legend states that during the reign of King [[Shō Taikyū]], a monk named Tsuruo, who had been training in Japan, made a vow to visit [[Kumano]] upon his successful return to Ryukyu. However, the king did not permit his travel. Tsuruo then had a divine dream where he was told to call out towards the northern mountains to find a sacred site. Following the dream's instructions, he found a spiritual site and reported it to the king, who then established the shrine. This legend is also mentioned in ''[[Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unranked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unranked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - Sueyoshi-gū] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] {{Shinto side}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sueyoshi-gū}} [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]]]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] == Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} ==References== Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - Sueyoshi-gū] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] {{Shinto side}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sueyoshi-gū}} [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]]]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] == Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} ==References== {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sueyoshi-gū | image = [[File:Sueyoshi Park Naha Okinawa Japan27s3.jpg|270px|Haiden]]
Haiden | location = 1-8 Sueyoshi-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 48.6 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 50.6 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Keitai era (1450–1457) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | other names = Shurishadan | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = November 23 | rituals = }} [[File:Sueyoshi-gū Honden.JPG|thumb|250px|main hall
Designated as a National Treasure in 1936, destroyed during the Pacific War, and rebuilt in 1972.]] [[File:Sueyoshigu.jpg|thumb|260x260px|View of Haiden from the side of the shrine office]] {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|26.230167|127.714056}}|caption=Sueyoshi-gū|width=256}} '''Sueyoshi-gū''' (末吉宮, Sueyoshi-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within [[Sueyoshi Park]] in [[Sueyoshi-cho]], [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are the [[Kumano Gongen]]: *'''[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]''' (いざなみのみこと) *'''[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]''' (はやたまをのみこと) *'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]''' (ことさかをのみこと) Additionally, the following deities are enshrined separately: *'''Tsuchi Mioya Kami''' (つちみおやかみ) *'''Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひこのみこと) *'''Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひめのみこと) *'''Ubusuna Kami''' (うぶすなのかみ) == History == According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Daikosan Manju-ji Temple, Sueyoshi-gū was founded during the [[Keitai era]] (1450–1457). The legend states that during the reign of King [[Shō Taikyū]], a monk named Tsuruo, who had been training in Japan, made a vow to visit [[Kumano]] upon his successful return to Ryukyu. However, the king did not permit his travel. Tsuruo then had a divine dream where he was told to call out towards the northern mountains to find a sacred site. Following the dream's instructions, he found a spiritual site and reported it to the king, who then established the shrine. This legend is also mentioned in ''[[Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unranked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unranked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} {{脚注ヘルプ}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} ==References== Sueyoshi-gū] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] {{Shinto side}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Sueyoshi-gū}} [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]]]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]] == Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} ==References==
