This legend is also mentioned in ''[[Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|12=draft}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|12=draft}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|12=draft}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|12=draft}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|12=draft}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=draft}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=draft}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=draft}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=draft}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=draft}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=draft}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unranked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unranked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - Sueyoshi-gū] {{Shinto side}} ==References== {{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]] [[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:末吉宮]] [[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Sueyoshi-gū}}

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sueyoshi-gū | image = [[File:Sueyoshi Park Naha Okinawa Japan27s3.jpg|270px|Haiden]]
Haiden | location = 1-8 Sueyoshi-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 48.6 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 50.6 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Keitai era (1450–1457) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | other names = Shurishadan | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = November 23 | rituals = }} [[File:Sueyoshi-gū Honden.JPG|thumb|250px|main hall
Designated as a National Treasure in 1936, destroyed during the Pacific War, and rebuilt in 1972.]] [[File:Sueyoshigu.jpg|thumb|260x260px|View of Haiden from the side of the shrine office]] {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|26.230167|127.714056}}|caption=Sueyoshi-gū|width=256}} '''Sueyoshi-gū''' (末吉宮, Sueyoshi-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within [[Sueyoshi Park]] in [[Sueyoshi-cho]], [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are the [[Kumano Gongen]]: *'''[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]''' (いざなみのみこと) *'''[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]''' (はやたまをのみこと) *'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]''' (ことさかをのみこと) Additionally, the following deities are enshrined separately: *'''Tsuchi Mioya Kami''' (つちみおやかみ) *'''Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひこのみこと) *'''Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひめのみこと) *'''Ubusuna Kami''' (うぶすなのかみ) == History == According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Daikosan Manju-ji Temple, Sueyoshi-gū was founded during the [[Keitai era]] (1450–1457). The legend states that during the reign of King [[Shō Taikyū]], a monk named Tsuruo, who had been training in Japan, made a vow to visit [[Kumano]] upon his successful return to Ryukyu. However, the king did not permit his travel. Tsuruo then had a divine dream where he was told to call out towards the northern mountains to find a sacred site. Following the dream's instructions, he found a spiritual site and reported it to the king, who then established the shrine. This legend is also mentioned in ''[[Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|12=draft}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|12=draft}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|12=draft}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|12=draft}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|12=draft}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=draft}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=draft}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=draft}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=draft}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=draft}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=draft}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=draft}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unranked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unranked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - Sueyoshi-gū] {{Shinto side}} ==References== {{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]] [[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:末吉宮]] [[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Sueyoshi-gū}}

At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - Sueyoshi-gū] {{Shinto side}} ==References== {{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]] [[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:末吉宮]] [[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Sueyoshi-gū}}

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sueyoshi-gū | image = [[File:Sueyoshi Park Naha Okinawa Japan27s3.jpg|270px|Haiden]]
Haiden | location = 1-8 Sueyoshi-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 48.6 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 50.6 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Keitai era (1450–1457) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | other names = Shurishadan | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = November 23 | rituals = }} [[File:Sueyoshi-gū Honden.JPG|thumb|250px|main hall
Designated as a National Treasure in 1936, destroyed during the Pacific War, and rebuilt in 1972.]] [[File:Sueyoshigu.jpg|thumb|260x260px|View of Haiden from the side of the shrine office]] {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|26.230167|127.714056}}|caption=Sueyoshi-gū|width=256}} '''Sueyoshi-gū''' (末吉宮, Sueyoshi-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within [[Sueyoshi Park]] in [[Sueyoshi-cho]], [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are the [[Kumano Gongen]]: *'''[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]''' (いざなみのみこと) *'''[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]''' (はやたまをのみこと) *'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]''' (ことさかをのみこと) Additionally, the following deities are enshrined separately: *'''Tsuchi Mioya Kami''' (つちみおやかみ) *'''Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひこのみこと) *'''Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひめのみこと) *'''Ubusuna Kami''' (うぶすなのかみ) == History == According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Daikosan Manju-ji Temple, Sueyoshi-gū was founded during the [[Keitai era]] (1450–1457). The legend states that during the reign of King [[Shō Taikyū]], a monk named Tsuruo, who had been training in Japan, made a vow to visit [[Kumano]] upon his successful return to Ryukyu. However, the king did not permit his travel. Tsuruo then had a divine dream where he was told to call out towards the northern mountains to find a sacred site. Following the dream's instructions, he found a spiritual site and reported it to the king, who then established the shrine. This legend is also mentioned in ''[[Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|12=draft|12=draft}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|12=draft|12=draft}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|12=draft|12=draft}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|12=draft|12=draft}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=draft|12=draft}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft|12=draft}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=draft|12=draft}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=draft|12=draft}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=draft|12=draft}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unranked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unranked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - Sueyoshi-gū] {{Shinto side}} ==References== {{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]] [[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:末吉宮]] [[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]] {{citation needed|Sueyoshi-gū}}

Designated as a National Treasure in 1936, destroyed during the Pacific War, and rebuilt in 1972.]] [[File:Sueyoshigu.jpg|thumb|260x260px|View of Haiden from the side of the shrine office]] {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|26.230167|127.714056}}|caption=Sueyoshi-gū|width=256}} '''Sueyoshi-gū''' (末吉宮, Sueyoshi-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within [[Sueyoshi Park]] in [[Sueyoshi-cho]], [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are the [[Kumano Gongen]]: *'''[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]''' (いざなみのみこと) *'''[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]''' (はやたまをのみこと) *'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]''' (ことさかをのみこと) Additionally, the following deities are enshrined separately: *'''Tsuchi Mioya Kami''' (つちみおやかみ) *'''Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひこのみこと) *'''Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひめのみこと) *'''Ubusuna Kami''' (うぶすなのかみ) == History == According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Daikosan Manju-ji Temple, Sueyoshi-gū was founded during the [[Keitai era]] (1450–1457). The legend states that during the reign of King [[Shō Taikyū]], a monk named Tsuruo, who had been training in Japan, made a vow to visit [[Kumano]] upon his successful return to Ryukyu. However, the king did not permit his travel. Tsuruo then had a divine dream where he was told to call out towards the northern mountains to find a sacred site. Following the dream's instructions, he found a spiritual site and reported it to the king, who then established the shrine. This legend is also mentioned in ''[[Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|12=draft|12=draft}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|12=draft|12=draft}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|12=draft|12=draft}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|12=draft|12=draft}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=draft|12=draft}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft|12=draft}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=draft|12=draft}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=draft|12=draft}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=draft|12=draft}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unranked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unranked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - Sueyoshi-gū] {{Shinto side}} ==References== {{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]] [[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:末吉宮]] [[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]] {{citation needed|Sueyoshi-gū}}

In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|12=draft|12=draft}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|12=draft|12=draft}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|12=draft|12=draft}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=draft|12=draft}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=draft|12=draft}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=draft|12=draft}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=draft|12=draft}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=draft|12=draft}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unRanked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unRanked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sueyoshi-gū | image = [[File:Sueyoshi Park Naha Okinawa Japan27s3.jpg|270px|Haiden]]
Haiden | location = 1-8 Sueyoshi-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 48.6 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 50.6 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine Rank = Former unRanked shrine | founded = Keitai era (1450–1457) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | other names = Shurishadan | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = November 23 | rituals = }} [[File:Sueyoshi-gū Honden.JPG|thumb|250px|main hall
Designated as a National Treasure in 1936, destroyed during the Pacific War, and rebuilt in 1972.]] [[File:Sueyoshigu.jpg|thumb|260x260px|View of Haiden from the side of the shrine office]] {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|26.230167|127.714056}}|caption=Sueyoshi-gū|width=256}} '''Sueyoshi-gū''' (末吉宮, Sueyoshi-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within [[Sueyoshi Park]] in [[Sueyoshi-cho]], [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unRanked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are the [[Kumano Gongen]]: *'''[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]''' (いざなみのみこと) *'''[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]''' (はやたまをのみこと) *'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]''' (ことさかをのみこと) Additionally, the following deities are enshrined separately: *'''Tsuchi Mioya Kami''' (つちみおやかみ) *'''Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひこのみこと) *'''Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひめのみこと) *'''Ubusuna Kami''' (うぶすなのかみ) == History == According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Daikosan Manju-ji Temple, Sueyoshi-gū was founded during the [[Keitai era]] (1450–1457). The legend states that during the reign of King [[Shō Taikyū]], a monk named Tsuruo, who had been training in Japan, made a vow to visit [[Kumano]] upon his successful return to Ryukyu. However, the king did not permit his travel. Tsuruo then had a divine dream where he was told to call out towards the northern mountains to find a sacred site. Following the dream's instructions, he found a spiritual site and reported it to the king, who then established the shrine. This legend is also mentioned in ''[[Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unRanked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unRanked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - Sueyoshi-gū] {{Shinto side}} ==References== {{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]] [[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:末吉宮]] [[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]] {{citation needed|Sueyoshi-gū}}

revision.comment: 45 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:末吉宮 up to rev 94116692
revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 末吉宮 |画像 = [[ファイル:Sueyoshi Park Naha Okinawa Japan27s3.jpg|270px|拝殿]]
拝殿 |所在地 = [[沖縄県]][[那覇市]]首里末吉町1-8 | 緯度度 = 26 | 緯度分 = 13 | 緯度秒 = 48.6 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 127 |経度分 = 42 | 経度秒 = 50.6 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP | 地図 = Japan Naha city |祭神 = [[伊弉冉神]]・[[速玉男尊]]・[[事解男尊]] |社格 = 旧無格社 |創建 = 景泰年間（1450年 - 1457年） |本殿 = [[流造]] |別名 = 首里社壇 |札所等 = 琉球八社 |例祭 = 11月23日 |神事 = }} [[ファイル:Sueyoshi-gū Honden.JPG|thumb|250px|本殿
昭和11年（1936年）に国宝の指定を受けていたが太平洋戦争により焼失し、昭和47年（1972年）に再建された。]] [[ファイル:Sueyoshigu.jpg|thumb|260x260px|社務所脇から見上げた拝殿]] {{Location map|Japan Mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|26.230167|127.714056}}|caption=末吉宮|width=256}} '''末吉宮'''（すえよしぐう）は、[[沖縄県]][[那覇市]][[首里末吉町]]の[[末吉公園]]内にある[[神社]]で[[琉球八社]]のひとつ。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は無格社。 == 祭神 == 主祭神として以下の[[熊野権現]]を祀っている。 *'''[[イザナミ|伊弉冉尊]]''' （いざなみのみこと） *'''[[速玉男尊]]''' （はやたまをのみこと） *'''[[事解男尊]]''' （こよさかをのみこと） また、別鎮斎として以下の神を祀っている。 *'''土祖神''' （つちみおやかみ） *'''澳津彦命''' （おくつひこのみこと） *'''澳津姫命''' （おくつひめのみこと） *'''産土神''' （うぶすなのかみ） == 由緒 == [[康熙]]52年（1713年、[[和暦]]では[[正徳 (日本)|正徳]]3年）に成立した『[[琉球国由来記]] 巻11 密門諸寺縁起』『琉球史料叢書 第1』1962年に所収。の〔大慶山万寿寺〕の段にある「大慶山権現縁起」では、当社の開基を[[景泰|景泰年間]]（[[1450年]] - [[1457年]]）であるとし、その創始について次のような伝承を記している。 [[第一尚氏|琉球王国]]の第6代国王[[尚泰久王]]の御世、天界寺の前住職であった鶴翁和尚が壮年の頃に[[倭国]]で修行していた際、[[熊野]]の方に向かって「もし、私が学道の修行を成就したなら、帰国して本意の後、熊野へ参詣致します」と誓って言った。やがて学道修行は成就し、帰国して住持となったことから、誓願を果たすべく尚泰久王に暇請いを願い出たが、王はこれを許さなかった。鶴翁和尚は頻りに暇請いをおこなったが、ある時、夢に人が現れ、「師よ志を遂げたいと欲するなら、是より北山に向かって大きな声で呼びなさい。応ずる所に霊験があるだろう。そこが即ち居所である。私は[[熊野権現]]である」と言った。夢より覚め、稀有に思いながらも一峰に到りて声を上げると前の山に響くところがあった。そこを尋ねたところ、崎嶇嶃岩（きくせんがん）としてあたかも霊地、人跡およばざる場所であった。そこに鬼面が一つあり、すなわち霊験であるとこれを拝んだ。このことを王殿に奏したところ、王もまた霊夢を見たことから、この出来事が意味なきことではないだろうと大臣等に詔し、この地に大社を建立した。また、鶴翁和尚がこの場所を歩いたとき1枚の鏡を獲たが、霊光を放つので社殿内陣に蔵した。 上記伝承は[[慶安]]元年（[[1648年]]）に初版が開板された『[[琉球神道記]] 巻第5』『琉球神道記』1943年。の「末好権現事」にも、ほぼ同様の内容が記載されている『琉球国由来記 巻11 密門諸寺縁起』の〔大慶山万寿寺〕の段には「神道記之説」と記されており、『琉球国由来記』が『琉球神道記』を参照していることを窺わせている。。 『琉球国由来記』に記述されている万寿寺（現・[[遍照寺 (沖縄市)|遍照寺]]）[[薩摩藩]]による琉球征伐（征縄の役）後に遍照寺へ改名させられた。は[[真言宗]]8公寺の一つとされた当社[[別当寺]]であったが、『琉球宗教史の研究』では当社建立と同時に万寿寺も建立されたのではないかと推測している『琉球宗教史の研究　第5編 明治の宗教政策　第1章 古琉球の社寺概観』より。『琉球宗教史の研究』1965年。。 当社は官社の制により琉球八社とされた。官社へは王府から神職の役俸並びに営繕費が支給されたが、当社へは神職として[[祝部]]・内侍・宮童が置かれた。また、祝部・内侍が[[神楽]]の際に着用する服装は全て王府の寺社座から支給を受けた『琉球宗教史の研究　第5編 明治の宗教政策　第2章 社寺禄の処分　第3節 社禄の処分（神社）』より。『琉球宗教史の研究』1965年。。 [[明治時代]]に入り、 [[沖縄県の歴史#琉球処分|琉球処分]]により琉球王国が廃され[[沖縄県]]が置かれると、当社は近代社格制度により無格社とされた。『琉球宗教史の研究』によれば、沖縄県行政府では当社を村社に列することを立案したが、経済的な理由から村社列格が出来ず、また社殿その他の設備においても不備な点が多々あって村社列格が事実上不可能であることから、とりあえず無格社として残置し、追々維持拡張整備して村社に引き直す根基を充実するよう努めることになったとしている『琉球宗教史の研究　第5編 明治の宗教政策　第4章 社寺組織の改革　第2節 神社組織の改革』より。『琉球宗教史の研究』1965年。。 さらに『琉球宗教史の研究』によれば、当時地元の民衆と信仰的に直接結合していたのは[[御嶽 (沖縄)|御嶽]]拝所であり、無格社となり日本政府の経済的保障がなかった当社は、その後経済的にも信仰的にも見るに耐えない無残な状態を呈することとなり、昭和14年（[[1939年]]）の沖縄県振興事業による復興計画に取り上げられた時には、明治末期頃まで残置していた拝殿は失せ、[[沖宮]]とともに[[昭和]]11年（[[1936年]]）[[国宝]]の指定を受けていた三間社流造の本殿加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日も屋根瓦はほとんど落ち、三方[[垂木]]のうち二方が失せ、全体的に腐朽荒廃してる状況であった『琉球宗教史の研究　第5編 明治の宗教政策　第5章 神社の列格並に創立　第1節 琉球八社』より。『琉球宗教史の研究』1965年。。 この本殿も激しい[[沖縄戦]]の中で砲撃を受け、建物の礎石、柱2本と[[梁 (建築)#梁の意匠|虹梁]]を残し爆散してしまった。現在見ることが出来る社殿は、残った礎石と資材を基に昭和47年（[[1972年]]）復元したものである社務所脇の銅板『史跡 末吉宮跡』による。。 == 文化財 == ;沖縄県指定有形文化財 *磴道 ;史跡 *末吉宮跡 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|author=袋中良定|authorlink=袋中|editor=明治聖徳記念学会|title=琉球神道記|series= |publisher=明世堂書店|year=1943|month=10}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor1-first=|editor1-last=伊波普猷|editor1-link=伊波普猷|editor2-first=|editor2-last=東恩納寛惇|editor2-link=東恩納寛惇|editor3-first=重|editor3-last=横山|editor3-link=横山重|title=琉球史料叢書 第1|series=琉球史料叢書|publisher=井上書房|year=1962|month=6}} ‐ 『琉球国由来記』巻1から巻11を所収。 * {{Cite book|和書|author=鳥越憲三郎|title=琉球宗教史の研究|series= |publisher=[[角川書店]]|year=1965|month=3}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=加治順人|year=2000|month=10|title=沖縄の神社|series= |publisher=ひるぎ社|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|和書|author=石上敏 |title=末吉宮論‐中世日本との関わりの中で‐ |series= |publisher=大阪商業大学論集　第180号 |date=2016-2 |isbn= }} == 関連項目 == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} *[[九州・沖縄地方の史跡一覧]] == 外部リンク == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index 国指定文化財等データベース] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html 沖縄県神社庁公式サイト　末吉宮] {{琉球八社}} {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:すえよしくう}} [[Category:沖縄県の神社]] [[Category:那覇市の建築物]] [[Category:那覇市の歴史]] [[Category:熊野神社]] [[Category:熊野信仰]] [[Category:沖縄県の旧無格社]] [[Category:沖縄県にある国指定の史跡]]

(沖縄)|御嶽]]拝所であり、無格社となり日本政府の経済的保障がなかった当社は、その後経済的にも信仰的にも見るに耐えない無残な状態を呈することとなり、昭和14年（[[1939年]]）の沖縄県振興事業による復興計画に取り上げられた時には、明治末期頃まで残置していた拝殿は失せ、[[沖宮]]とともに[[昭和]]11年（[[1936年]]）[[国宝]]の指定を受けていた三間社流造の本殿加治順人『沖縄の神社』p143　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日も屋根瓦はほとんど落ち、三方[[垂木]]のうち二方が失せ、全体的に腐朽荒廃してる状況であった『琉球宗教史の研究　第5編 明治の宗教政策　第5章 神社の列格並に創立　第1節 琉球八社』より。『琉球宗教史の研究』1965年。。 この本殿も激しい[[沖縄戦]]の中で砲撃を受け、建物の礎石、柱2本と[[梁 (建築)#梁の意匠|虹梁]]を残し爆散してしまった。現在見ることが出来る社殿は、残った礎石と資材を基に昭和47年（[[1972年]]）復元したものである社務所脇の銅板『史跡 末吉宮跡』による。。 == 文化財 == ;沖縄県指定有形文化財 *磴道 ;史跡 *末吉宮跡 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite book|和書|author=袋中良定|authorlink=袋中|editor=明治聖徳記念学会|title=琉球神道記|series= |publisher=明世堂書店|year=1943|month=10}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor1-first=|editor1-last=伊波普猷|editor1-link=伊波普猷|editor2-first=|editor2-last=東恩納寛惇|editor2-link=東恩納寛惇|editor3-first=重|editor3-last=横山|editor3-link=横山重|title=琉球史料叢書 第1|series=琉球史料叢書|publisher=井上書房|year=1962|month=6}} ‐ 『琉球国由来記』巻1から巻11を所収。 * {{Cite book|和書|author=鳥越憲三郎|title=琉球宗教史の研究|series= |publisher=[[角川書店]]|year=1965|month=3}} * {{Cite book|和書|author=加治順人|year=2000|month=10|title=沖縄の神社|series= |publisher=ひるぎ社|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|和書|author=石上敏 |title=末吉宮論‐中世日本との関わりの中で‐ |series= |publisher=大阪商業大学論集　第180号 |date=2016-2 |isbn= }} == {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sueyoshi-gū | image = [[File:Sueyoshi Park Naha Okinawa Japan27s3.jpg|270px|Haiden]]
Haiden | location = 1-8 Sueyoshi-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 48.6 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 50.6 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine Rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Keitai era (1450–1457) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | other names = Shurishadan | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = November 23 | rituals = }} [[File:Sueyoshi-gū Honden.JPG|thumb|250px|main hall
Designated as a National Treasure in 1936, destroyed during the Pacific War, and rebuilt in 1972.]] [[File:Sueyoshigu.jpg|thumb|260x260px|View of Haiden from the side of the shrine office]] {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|26.230167|127.714056}}|caption=Sueyoshi-gū|width=256}} '''Sueyoshi-gū''' (末吉宮, Sueyoshi-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within [[Sueyoshi Park]] in [[Sueyoshi-cho]], [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are the [[Kumano Gongen]]: *'''[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]''' (いざなみのみこと) *'''[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]''' (はやたまをのみこと) *'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]''' (ことさかをのみこと) Additionally, the following deities are enshrined separately: *'''Tsuchi Mioya Kami''' (つちみおやかみ) *'''Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひこのみこと) *'''Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひめのみこと) *'''Ubusuna Kami''' (うぶすなのかみ) == History == According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Daikosan Manju-ji Temple, Sueyoshi-gū was founded during the [[Keitai era]] (1450–1457). The legend states that during the reign of King [[Shō Taikyū]], a monk named Tsuruo, who had been training in Japan, made a vow to visit [[Kumano]] upon his successful return to Ryukyu. However, the king did not permit his travel. Tsuruo then had a divine dream where he was told to call out towards the northern mountains to find a sacred site. Following the dream's instructions, he found a spiritual site and reported it to the king, who then established the shrine. This legend is also mentioned in ''[[Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unranked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unranked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - Sueyoshi-gū] {{Shinto side}} ==References== {{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]] [[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:末吉宮]] [[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]] {{citation needed|Sueyoshi-gū}} {{translated page|ja|末吉宮|version=94116692|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sueyoshi-gū | image = [[File:Sueyoshi Park Naha Okinawa Japan27s3.jpg|270px|Haiden]]
Haiden | location = 1-8 Sueyoshi-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 48.6 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 50.6 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]
[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine Rank = Former unranked shrine | founded = Keitai era (1450–1457) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | other names = Shurishadan | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = November 23 | rituals = }} [[File:Sueyoshi-gū Honden.JPG|thumb|250px|main hall
Designated as a National Treasure in 1936, destroyed during the Pacific War, and rebuilt in 1972.]] [[File:Sueyoshigu.jpg|thumb|260x260px|View of Haiden from the side of the shrine office]] {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|26.230167|127.714056}}|caption=Sueyoshi-gū|width=256}} '''Sueyoshi-gū''' (末吉宮, Sueyoshi-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located within [[Sueyoshi Park]] in [[Sueyoshi-cho]], [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are the [[Kumano Gongen]]: *'''[[Izanami-no-Mikoto]]''' (いざなみのみこと) *'''[[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]''' (はやたまをのみこと) *'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]''' (ことさかをのみこと) Additionally, the following deities are enshrined separately: *'''Tsuchi Mioya Kami''' (つちみおやかみ) *'''Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひこのみこと) *'''Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひめのみこと) *'''Ubusuna Kami''' (うぶすなのかみ) == History == According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Daikosan Manju-ji Temple, Sueyoshi-gū was founded during the [[Keitai era]] (1450–1457). The legend states that during the reign of King [[Shō Taikyū]], a monk named Tsuruo, who had been training in Japan, made a vow to visit [[Kumano]] upon his successful return to Ryukyu. However, the king did not permit his travel. Tsuruo then had a divine dream where he was told to call out towards the northern mountains to find a sacred site. Following the dream's instructions, he found a spiritual site and reported it to the king, who then established the shrine. This legend is also mentioned in ''[[Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. After the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[Meiji period]], with the [[Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unranked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unranked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - Sueyoshi-gū] {{Shinto side}} ==References== {{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{citation needed|Sueyoshi-gū}} {{translated page|ja|末吉宮|version=94116692|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[sn:Sueyoshi-gū]] [[en:Sueyoshi-gū]] [[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:末吉宮]]
