It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are the [[Kumano Gongen]]: *'''[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]]''' (いざなみのみこと) *'''[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]''' (はやたまをのみこと) *'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]''' (ことさかをのみこと) Additionally, the following deities are enshrined separately: *'''Tsuchi Mioya Kami''' (つちみおやかみ) *'''Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひこのみこと) *'''Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひめのみこと) *'''Ubusuna Kami''' (うぶすなのかみ) == History == According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Daikosan Manju-ji Temple, Sueyoshi-gū was founded during the [[Keitai era]] (1450–1457). The legend states that during the reign of King [[:en:Shō Taikyū|Shō Taikyū]], a monk named Tsuruo, who had been training in Japan, made a vow to visit [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]] upon his successful return to Ryukyu. However, the king did not permit his travel. Tsuruo then had a divine dream where he was told to call out towards the northern mountains to find a sacred site. Following the dream's instructions, he found a spiritual site and reported it to the king, who then established the shrine. This legend is also mentioned in ''[[:en:Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[:en:Shingon sect|Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], with the [[:en:Ryukyu Disposition|Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unranked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unranked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - Sueyoshi-gū] {{Shinto side}} ==References== {{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|末吉宮|version=94116692|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:末吉宮]] [[Category:National historic sites in Okinawa prefecture]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Sueyoshi-gū}} [[zh:末吉宮]] {{Commons category|Sueyoshi-gū}} [[zh:末吉宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11519653}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11519653}} [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]

[[sn:commons:Category:Sueyoshi-gū]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[ja:末吉宮]][[zh:末吉宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11519653]] Wikidata name: Sueyoshi-gū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sueyoshi-gū | image = File:Sueyoshi Park Naha Okinawa Japan27s3.jpg | location = 1-8 Sueyoshi-cho, [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 48.6 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 50.6 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]]
[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine Rank = Former Unranked shrine | founded = Keitai era (1450–1457) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | other names = Shurishadan | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = November 23 | rituals = | location = {{ill|Shuri Sueyoshichō|WD=Q11667116}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.230167|N|127.714056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | part_of = {{ill|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|WD=Q11573652}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }} [[File:Sueyoshi-gū Honden.JPG|thumb|250px|main hall
Designated as a National Treasure in 1936, destroyed during the Pacific War, and rebuilt in 1972.]] [[File:Sueyoshigu.jpg|thumb|260x260px|View of Haiden from the side of the shrine office]] {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|26.230167|127.714056}}|caption=Sueyoshi-gū|width=256}} '''Sueyoshi-gū''' (末吉宮, Sueyoshi-gū) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located within [[Sueyoshi Park]] in [[Sueyoshi-cho]], [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are the [[Kumano Gongen]]: *'''[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]]''' (いざなみのみこと) *'''[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]''' (はやたまをのみこと) *'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]''' (ことさかをのみこと) Additionally, the following deities are enshrined separately: *'''Tsuchi Mioya Kami''' (つちみおやかみ) *'''Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひこのみこと) *'''Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひめのみこと) *'''Ubusuna Kami''' (うぶすなのかみ) == History == According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Daikosan Manju-ji Temple, Sueyoshi-gū was founded during the [[Keitai era]] (1450–1457). The legend states that during the reign of King [[:en:Shō Taikyū|Shō Taikyū]], a monk named Tsuruo, who had been training in Japan, made a vow to visit [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]] upon his successful return to Ryukyu. However, the king did not permit his travel. Tsuruo then had a divine dream where he was told to call out towards the northern mountains to find a sacred site. Following the dream's instructions, he found a spiritual site and reported it to the king, who then established the shrine. This legend is also mentioned in ''[[:en:Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[:en:Shingon sect|Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], with the [[:en:Ryukyu Disposition|Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unranked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unranked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - Sueyoshi-gū] {{Shinto side}} ==References== {{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|末吉宮|version=94116692|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:末吉宮]] [[Category:National historic sites in Okinawa prefecture]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Sueyoshi-gū}} [[zh:末吉宮]] {{Commons category|Sueyoshi-gū}} [[zh:末吉宮]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] {{wikidata link|Q11519653}}

However, the king did not permit his travel. Tsuruo then had a divine dream where he was told to call out towards the northern mountains to find a sacred site. Following the dream's instructions, he found a spiritual site and reported it to the king, who then established the shrine. This legend is also mentioned in ''[[:en:Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[:en:Shingon sect|Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], with the [[:en:Ryukyu Disposition|Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unranked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unranked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine Rank = Former Unranked shrine | founded = Keitai era (1450–1457) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | other names = Shurishadan | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = November 23 | rituals = | location = {{ill|Shuri Sueyoshichō|WD=Q11667116}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.230167|N|127.714056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | part_of = {{ill|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|WD=Q11573652}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }} [[File:Sueyoshi-gū Honden.JPG|thumb|250px|main hall
Designated as a National Treasure in 1936, destroyed during the Pacific War, and rebuilt in 1972.]] [[File:Sueyoshigu.jpg|thumb|260x260px|View of Haiden from the side of the shrine office]] {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|26.230167|127.714056}}|caption=Sueyoshi-gū|width=256}} '''Sueyoshi-gū''' (末吉宮, Sueyoshi-gū) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located within [[Sueyoshi Park]] in [[Sueyoshi-cho]], [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are the [[Kumano Gongen]]: *'''[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]]''' (いざなみのみこと) *'''[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]''' (はやたまをのみこと) *'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]''' (ことさかをのみこと) Additionally, the following deities are enshrined separately: *'''Tsuchi Mioya Kami''' (つちみおやかみ) *'''Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひこのみこと) *'''Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひめのみこと) *'''Ubusuna Kami''' (うぶすなのかみ) == History == According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Daikosan Manju-ji Temple, Sueyoshi-gū was founded during the [[Keitai era]] (1450–1457). The legend states that during the reign of King [[:en:Shō Taikyū|Shō Taikyū]], a monk named Tsuruo, who had been training in Japan, made a vow to visit [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]] upon his successful return to Ryukyu. However, the king did not permit his travel. Tsuruo then had a divine dream where he was told to call out towards the northern mountains to find a sacred site. Following the dream's instructions, he found a spiritual site and reported it to the king, who then established the shrine. This legend is also mentioned in ''[[:en:Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[:en:Shingon sect|Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], with the [[:en:Ryukyu Disposition|Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unranked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unranked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. Following the dream's instructions, he found a spiritual site and reported it to the king, who then established the shrine. This legend is also mentioned in ''[[:en:Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[:en:Shingon sect|Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], with the [[:en:Ryukyu Disposition|Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unranked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unranked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - Sueyoshi-gū] {{Shinto side}} ==References== {{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|末吉宮|version=94116692|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:末吉宮]] [[Category:National historic sites in Okinawa prefecture]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Sueyoshi-gū}} [[zh:末吉宮]] {{Commons category|Sueyoshi-gū}} [[zh:末吉宮]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11519653}}

[[commons:Category:Sueyoshi-gū]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[ja:末吉宮]][[zh:末吉宮]] [[commons:Category:Sueyoshi-gū]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[ja:末吉宮]][[zh:末吉宮]] [[sn:commons:Category:Sueyoshi-gū]][[en:Sueyoshi-gū]][[ja:末吉宮]][[zh:末吉宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11519653]] Wikidata name: Sueyoshi-gū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Sueyoshi-gū | image = File:Sueyoshi Park Naha Okinawa Japan27s3.jpg | location = 1-8 Sueyoshi-cho, [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 13 | type seconds = 48.6 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 42 | longitude seconds = 50.6 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]]
[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine Rank = Former Unranked shrine | founded = Keitai era (1450–1457) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | other names = Shurishadan | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = November 23 | rituals = | location = {{ill|Shuri Sueyoshichō|WD=Q11667116}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.230167|N|127.714056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | part_of = {{ill|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|WD=Q11573652}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }} [[File:Sueyoshi-gū Honden.JPG|thumb|250px|main hall
Designated as a National Treasure in 1936, destroyed during the Pacific War, and rebuilt in 1972.]] [[File:Sueyoshigu.jpg|thumb|260x260px|View of Haiden from the side of the shrine office]] {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|26.230167|127.714056}}|caption=Sueyoshi-gū|width=256}} '''Sueyoshi-gū''' (末吉宮, Sueyoshi-gū) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located within [[Sueyoshi Park]] in [[Sueyoshi-cho]], [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are the [[Kumano Gongen]]: *'''[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]]''' (いざなみのみこと) *'''[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]''' (はやたまをのみこと) *'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]''' (ことさかをのみこと) Additionally, the following deities are enshrined separately: *'''Tsuchi Mioya Kami''' (つちみおやかみ) *'''Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひこのみこと) *'''Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひめのみこと) *'''Ubusuna Kami''' (うぶすなのかみ) == History == According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Daikosan Manju-ji Temple, Sueyoshi-gū was founded during the [[Keitai era]] (1450–1457). The legend states that during the reign of King [[:en:Shō Taikyū|Shō Taikyū]], a monk named Tsuruo, who had been training in Japan, made a vow to visit [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]] upon his successful return to Ryukyu. However, the king did not permit his travel. Tsuruo then had a divine dream where he was told to call out towards the northern mountains to find a sacred site. Following the dream's instructions, he found a spiritual site and reported it to the king, who then established the shrine. This legend is also mentioned in ''[[:en:Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[:en:Shingon sect|Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], with the [[:en:Ryukyu Disposition|Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unranked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unranked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are the [[Kumano Gongen]]: *'''[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]]''' (いざなみのみこと) *'''[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]''' (はやたまをのみこと) *'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]''' (ことさかをのみこと) Additionally, the following deities are enshrined separately: *'''Tsuchi Mioya Kami''' (つちみおやかみ) *'''Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひこのみこと) *'''Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひめのみこと) *'''Ubusuna Kami''' (うぶすなのかみ) == History == According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Daikosan Manju-ji Temple, Sueyoshi-gū was founded during the [[Keitai era]] (1450–1457). The legend states that during the reign of King [[:en:Shō Taikyū|Shō Taikyū]], a monk named Tsuruo, who had been training in Japan, made a vow to visit [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]] upon his successful return to Ryukyu. However, the king did not permit his travel. Tsuruo then had a divine dream where he was told to call out towards the northern mountains to find a sacred site. Following the dream's instructions, he found a spiritual site and reported it to the king, who then established the shrine. This legend is also mentioned in ''[[:en:Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[:en:Shingon sect|Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], with the [[:en:Ryukyu Disposition|Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unranked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unranked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - Sueyoshi-gū] {{Shinto side}} ==References== {{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|末吉宮|version=94116692|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:末吉宮]] {{Commons category|Sueyoshi-gū}} [[zh:末吉宮]] {{Commons category|Sueyoshi-gū}} [[zh:末吉宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11519653}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:National historic sites in Okinawa prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]
[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]] | shrine Rank = Former Unranked shrine | founded = Keitai era (1450–1457) | main hall = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | other names = Shurishadan | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = November 23 | rituals = | location = {{ill|Shuri Sueyoshichō|WD=Q11667116}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.230167|N|127.714056|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | part_of = {{ill|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|WD=Q11573652}} | architecture_style = {{ill|nagare-zukuri|WD=Q210684}} }} [[File:Sueyoshi-gū Honden.JPG|thumb|250px|main hall
Designated as a National Treasure in 1936, destroyed during the Pacific War, and rebuilt in 1972.]] [[File:Sueyoshigu.jpg|thumb|260x260px|View of Haiden from the side of the shrine office]] {{location map|Japan mapplot|coordinates={{Coord|26.230167|127.714056}}|caption=Sueyoshi-gū|width=256}} '''Sueyoshi-gū''' (末吉宮, Sueyoshi-gū) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located within [[Sueyoshi Park]] in [[Sueyoshi-cho]], [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] and was classified as an unranked shrine in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]]. == Deities == The main deities enshrined are the [[Kumano Gongen]]: *'''[[:en:Izanami|Izanami]]''' (いざなみのみこと) *'''[[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]]''' (はやたまをのみこと) *'''[[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]''' (ことさかをのみこと) Additionally, the following deities are enshrined separately: *'''Tsuchi Mioya Kami''' (つちみおやかみ) *'''Okutsuhiko-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひこのみこと) *'''Okutsuhime-no-Mikoto''' (おくつひめのみこと) *'''Ubusuna Kami''' (うぶすなのかみ) == History == According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' Volume 11, section on Daikosan Manju-ji Temple, Sueyoshi-gū was founded during the [[Keitai era]] (1450–1457). The legend states that during the reign of King [[:en:Shō Taikyū|Shō Taikyū]], a monk named Tsuruo, who had been training in Japan, made a vow to visit [[:en:Kumano|Kumano]] upon his successful return to Ryukyu. However, the king did not permit his travel. Tsuruo then had a divine dream where he was told to call out towards the northern mountains to find a sacred site. Following the dream's instructions, he found a spiritual site and reported it to the king, who then established the shrine. This legend is also mentioned in ''[[:en:Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki]]'' Volume 5, published in 1648''Ryūkyū Shintō-ki'', 1943.. The temple associated with the shrine, Manju-ji (now [[Penjo-ji Temple]]), was one of the eight main temples of the [[:en:Shingon sect|Shingon sect]] and was likely established simultaneously with the shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 1, 1965.. After the [[:en:Invasion of Ryukyu|Invasion of Ryukyu]] in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by [[:en:Kikoe-ōgimi|Kikoe-ōgimi]] and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in [[:en:Shō Hō|1625]], it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a [[:en:Shō Ken|New]] Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested [[:en:Shō Shōken|Shō Shōken]] to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are [[:en:Naminoue Shrine|Naminoue Shrine]], {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, [[:en:Hachiman shrine|Asato-Hachimangū‎]], {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} Under the government system, Sueyoshi-gū was designated one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines, receiving financial support from the royal government for the priests and shrine upkeep''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 2, Section 3, 1965.. During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], with the [[:en:Ryukyu Disposition|Ryukyu Disposition]] and the establishment of Okinawa Prefecture, the shrine was classified as an unranked shrine. Due to economic constraints and inadequate facilities, it could not be upgraded to a village shrine but was left as an independent unranked shrine''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 4, Section 2, 1965.. In 1939, a restoration plan for the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was implemented as part of Okinawa Prefecture's revitalization project. At that time, the shrine was in a state of disrepair, having lost its roof tiles and most of its structure. The main hall, designated a National Treasure in 1936, was destroyed during the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]''Research on Ryukyu Religious History'', Vol. 5, Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965.. The current structure was rebuilt in 1972 using the remaining foundation stones and materialsInformation board at the shrine office: "Historical Site Sueyoshi-gū Ruins".. == Cultural Properties == ;Okinawa Prefecture Designated Tangible Cultural Property *Stone steps ;Historic Sites *Sueyoshi-gū Ruins == References == {{Reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|author=Nakachu Ryotei|editor=Meiji Jingu Shrine Research Institute|title=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|publisher=Meiseido Shoten|year=1943|month=October}} * {{Cite book|editor1-first=Hokusei|editor1-last=Iha|editor2-first=Kanju|editor2-last=Higashionna|editor3-first=Shige|editor3-last=Yokoyama|title=Ryukyu Historical Documents Series Volume 1|publisher=Inoue Shobo|year=1962|month=June}} - Contains Volumes 1 to 11 of ''Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom''. * {{Cite book|author=Kenzaburo Torigoe|title=Research on Ryukyu Religious History|publisher=[[Kadokawa Shoten]]|year=1965|month=March}} * {{Cite book|author=Junhito Kaji|year=2000|month=October|title=Shrines of Okinawa|publisher=Hirugi-sha|isbn= }} * {{Cite book|author=Toshi Ishigami |title=Sueyoshi-gū and Its Connections with Medieval Japan |series=Osaka University of Commerce Bulletin No. 180 |date=February 2016|isbn= }} == Related Pages == {{Commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} * [[List of Historic Sites of Japan (Kyushu and Okinawa)]] == External Links == *[https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/bsys/index Database of Nationally Designated Cultural Properties] *[http://jinjacho.naminouegu.jp/sueyoshi.html Official Site of Okinawa Prefecture Shrine Association - Sueyoshi-gū] {{Shinto side}} ==References== {{commonscat|Sueyoshi-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|末吉宮|version=94116692|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[zh:%E6%9C%AB%E5%90%89%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:末吉宮]] {{Commons category|Sueyoshi-gū}} [[zh:末吉宮]] {{Commons category|Sueyoshi-gū}} [[zh:末吉宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11519653}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:National historic sites in Okinawa prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]]
